title: Caprese
subtitle: Fresh tomatoes, buffalo mozzarella, basil, and olive oil.
excerpt: Ripe tomatoes sliced and layered with fresh buffalo mozzarella, scattered with basil leaves, and dressed with nothing but good olive oil and sea salt. A salad that exists entirely to demonstrate the quality of its ingredients.
sourceNotes: Insalata caprese originates from the island of Capri off the Amalfi coast and appears in Italian recipe collections from the mid-twentieth century, though the combination of tomato, mozzarella, and basil is likely older. It was historically a showcase of Campanian produce: the tomatoes, mozzarella di bufala from the buffalo farms of the Campania plain, and fresh basil. In its traditional form no vinegar is used, only olive oil, because the acid in the tomatoes is sufficient. The dish entered British restaurant menus in the 1980s and is now made widely at home.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to an hour before serving; do not add salt until just before serving or the tomatoes will release liquid.

content.text: Caprese is an ingredient-quality test with no technique to hide behind. The tomatoes must be ripe: firm, flavourless tomatoes produce a dish that tastes of nothing, no matter how good the mozzarella. Use buffalo mozzarella rather than cow mozzarella — the flavour and texture are different in the way that matters here.

There is no balsamic vinegar in a traditional caprese. Balsamic has become a common addition in British versions, but the dish as made on Capri uses only olive oil. The tomato provides all the acidity needed.

Slice the {{tomato}} tomatoes — larger ones in rounds, small ones halved or quartered. Drain the {{mozzarella-di-bufala}} of mozzarella and tear it into pieces rather than slicing. Arrange the tomatoes and mozzarella on a plate, alternating and overlapping. Scatter the {{basil}} leaves over the top. Drizzle with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of flaky salt and a little black pepper. Serve immediately or within an hour.

items.symptom: Dish is watery on the plate
items.cause: Tomatoes were salted too early, or mozzarella not drained.
items.fix: Add salt immediately before serving. Drain the mozzarella well and pat dry if it is very wet.

items.cause: Cow mozzarella used instead of buffalo, or mozzarella served cold from the fridge.
items.fix: Use mozzarella di bufala and let it come to room temperature for at least 30 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Bought from a fridge or out of season.
items.fix: Tomatoes should be stored and served at room temperature, never refrigerated. Out-of-season tomatoes cannot be improved by this preparation.

items.symptom: Basil has wilted and turned dark
items.cause: Added too early or bruised by cutting.
items.fix: Tear basil rather than cutting it, and add just before serving.

content.text: Caprese is the most straightforward application of a principle that runs through Italian cooking: that the best dish from available ingredients is the one that puts them on a plate and gets out of the way. The combination of tomato, mozzarella, and basil represents the colours of the Italian flag, which has been noted often enough to become a cliché; more useful is the observation that the combination also represents the three dominant flavours of Campanian summer cooking. It requires nothing from the cook except good judgement about ingredient quality.
